summary:
Louis XIV of France (r. 1643–1715, personal rule from 1661) was the longest-reigning monarch in European history and the archetype of absolute monarchy — the 'Sun King' whose 72-year reign made France the dominant power in Europe, imposed a new standard of royal grandeur and cultural patronage that all European courts imitated, and whose centralizing policies set the stage for the fiscal crisis that would eventually trigger the [[event:107|French Revolution]]. Louis's personal rule began when his first minister Cardinal Mazarin died in 1661; Louis promptly declared he would govern alone, refusing to appoint another prime minister. His administrative program dismantled the power of the French nobility by requiring nobles to attend his court at [[event:2503|Versailles]] — effectively making them permanent supplicants who competed for royal favor rather than building regional power bases.

Versailles — the magnificent palace complex Louis constructed at enormous expense from the 1660s onwards — was both a residence and a political instrument: its rigid court etiquette, choreographed royal ceremonies, and the presence of thousands of courtiers created a theater of royal power that projected an image of divine sovereignty. Louis's wars of expansion (the War of Devolution, the Dutch War, the Nine Years' War, and the [[event:2347|War of Spanish Succession]]) sought to extend French borders to the Rhine and ultimately the Pyrenees but exhausted French finances and united Europe against him. His revocation of the Edict of Nantes in 1685 (the [[event:2379|Edict]] had granted religious toleration to Protestants) expelled hundreds of thousands of skilled Huguenot craftsmen and merchants, devastating French manufacturing while enriching Protestant competitors. His cultural patronage — the Académie Française, the Comédie-Française, the support of Molière, Racine, and Lully — made French the language of European aristocracy and diplomacy for the following century.
